quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ionic bond"?

options.text: A strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F atom
Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer; typically between metals and nonmetals (ΔEN > 1.7)
A method for calculating equilibrium concentrations: Initial concentration, Change in concentration, Equilibrium concentration
The point where the indicator changes color; ideally matches the equivalence point; slightly different in practice

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ionic bond" is defined as: Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer; typically between metals and nonmetals (ΔEN > 1.7). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Covalent bond"?

options.text: Equilibrium constant for weak acid dissociation: HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻; larger Ka = stronger acid
A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass
A bond formed by sharing electron pairs between atoms; can be single (2e⁻), double (4e⁻), or triple (6e⁻)
The experimental measurement of heat changes using an insulated container; q = mcΔT for solution calorimetry

quiz_questions.explanation: "Covalent bond" is defined as: A bond formed by sharing electron pairs between atoms; can be single (2e⁻), double (4e⁻), or triple (6e⁻). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Lewis structure"?

options.text: Adding a species shifts equilibrium away from it; removing a species shifts equilibrium toward it
-log[H⁺]; a logarithmic scale where each unit represents a 10-fold change in [H⁺]; pH 3 is 1000× more acidic than pH 6
A mixture of two or more metals (or a metal and nonmetal); substitutional alloys have similar-sized atoms, interstitial alloys have small atoms in gaps
A diagram showing bonding pairs and lone pairs of electrons; follows the octet rule (8e⁻ for most atoms, 2e⁻ for H)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Lewis structure" is defined as: A diagram showing bonding pairs and lone pairs of electrons; follows the octet rule (8e⁻ for most atoms, 2e⁻ for H). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Formal charge"?

options.text: Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
FC = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); the best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges
An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product
A visual representation showing the relative enthalpies of reactants, products, and intermediates; illustrates exothermic/endothermic steps

quiz_questions.explanation: "Formal charge" is defined as: FC = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); the best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Bond polarity"?

options.text: When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance
A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon)
Unequal sharing of electrons in a covalent bond due to electronegativity difference; creates a dipole moment (δ⁺ and δ⁻)
Preventing corrosion by making the metal structure the cathode; achieved with a sacrificial anode (Zn, Mg) or impressed current

quiz_questions.explanation: "Bond polarity" is defined as: Unequal sharing of electrons in a covalent bond due to electronegativity difference; creates a dipole moment (δ⁺ and δ⁻). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions formed by electron transfer; typically between metals and nonmetals (ΔEN > 1.7)"?

options.text: Second law of thermodynamics
Solubility product (Ksp)
Ionic bond
Colligative properties

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ionic bond".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A bond formed by sharing electron pairs between atoms; can be single (2e⁻), double (4e⁻), or triple (6e⁻)"?

options.text: Covalent bond
Stoichiometric conversion
E°cell
Integrated rate law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Covalent bond".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A diagram showing bonding pairs and lone pairs of electrons; follows the octet rule (8e⁻ for most atoms, 2e⁻ for H)"?

options.text: pOH
Mole ratio
Lewis structure
Average bond energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Lewis structure".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "FC = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); the best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges"?

options.text: E°cell
Polyprotic acid
Hess's Law
Formal charge

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Formal charge".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Unequal sharing of electrons in a covalent bond due to electronegativity difference; creates a dipole moment (δ⁺ and δ⁻)"?

options.text: Factors affecting rate
Zwitterion
Conjugate pair
Bond polarity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Bond polarity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Chemical Bonding is accurate?

options.text: K is dimensionless and temperature-dependent.
Chemical Bonding connects atoms through ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.
Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.
Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Bonding as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Chemical Bonding?

options.text: Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).
Adding reactant or removing product shifts equilibrium forward (toward products); adding product or removing reactant shifts it in reverse.
Ionic bonds form when electrons transfer from metals to nonmetals (large electronegativity difference > 1.7), creating ions held by electrostatic attraction.
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Bonding as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Chemical Bonding?

options.text: Acid-Base Definitions have evolved from Arrhenius (acids produce H⁺, bases produce OH⁻ in water) to Brønsted-Lowry (acids donate H⁺, bases accept H⁺) to Lewis (acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs).

Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
Weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka/Kb values <1.
Covalent bonds share electron pairs between nonmetals, with bond polarity determined by electronegativity difference.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Bonding as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Chemical Bonding?

options.text: K is dimensionless and temperature-dependent.
The 5% approximation simplifies calculations when [HA]₀/Ka > 400.
Activation Energy and Catalysts are central to understanding and controlling reaction rates.
Lewis structures show bonding and lone pairs, with formal charge minimization guiding the best structure.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Bonding as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Chemical Bonding?

options.text: Atoms consist of protons (p⁺, +1 charge, ~1 amu) and neutrons (n⁰, neutral, ~1 amu) in the nucleus, surrounded by electrons (e⁻, -1 charge, ~1/1836 amu) in orbitals.
Calorimetry measures ΔH experimentally: in coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q = mcΔT; in bomb calorimetry (constant volume), q = CcalΔT.
Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure, vapor pressure lowering) depend on solute particle concentration, not identity.
Bond order, length, and energy are inversely/directly related.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Chemical Bonding as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Chemical Bonding?

options.text: Table salt (NaCl) as a classic ionic compound: Na donates one electron to Cl, forming Na⁺Cl⁻ with a crystal lattice structure and high melting point (801°C)
Ocean buffering against acidification: carbonate/bicarbonate system absorbing excess CO₂, but capacity being overwhelmed by current emission rates
Freeze-drying (lyophilization) of food and pharmaceuticals using sublimation under reduced pressure to remove water while preserving structure and nutrients
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chemical Bonding.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Chemical Bonding?

options.text: Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old
Diamond vs. graphite: same element (carbon) with different covalent bonding networks—diamond (4 bonds, 3D tetrahedral) is the hardest material, graphite (3 bonds, layered sheets) is a lubricant
Cold packs using endothermic dissolution of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) in water to absorb heat and cool injuries
Green chemistry principles in industry: catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes (100% atom economy) preferred over reactions producing significant waste byproducts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chemical Bonding.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Chemical Bonding applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Antifreeze (ethylene glycol) in car radiators both lowering freezing point and raising boiling point of the coolant solution
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)
Industrial ammonia production optimizing the ratio of N₂ to H₂ to minimize waste and maximize yield in the Haber process
Polar covalent bonds in water enabling hydrogen bonding, which gives water its uniquely high boiling point (100°C vs. H₂S at -60°C)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chemical Bonding.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Chemical Bonding?

options.text: Table salt (NaCl) as a classic ionic compound: Na donates one electron to Cl, forming Na⁺Cl⁻ with a crystal lattice structure and high melting point (801°C)
Industrial chemical production (ammonia synthesis: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃) using stoichiometry to optimize raw material usage and minimize waste
Soda carbonation: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq) equilibrium maintained under pressure; opening the bottle shifts equilibrium toward gas release (fizzing)
Gecko adhesion: billions of tiny setae on gecko feet exploit London dispersion forces to adhere to surfaces, inspiring synthetic 'gecko tape' technologies

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chemical Bonding.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Chemical Bonding in practice?

options.text: Rocket fuel calculations: precise stoichiometry of liquid H₂ and O₂ (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O) ensures maximum thrust without wasting expensive fuel
Diamond vs. graphite: same element (carbon) with different covalent bonding networks—diamond (4 bonds, 3D tetrahedral) is the hardest material, graphite (3 bonds, layered sheets) is a lubricant
Chain reaction mechanisms in combustion: initiation produces radicals, propagation sustains the reaction, termination removes radicals (relevant to engine knocking and explosives)
Quality control in pharmaceutical manufacturing: acid-base titrations determining the exact concentration of active ingredients in drug formulations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Chemical Bonding.
